title: Some Useful Signal Operations
summary: This page illustrates time scaling operations on a signal x(t), demonstrating both time compression and time expansion. It shows that x(3t) compresses the signal by a factor of 3, while x(t/2) expands the signal by a factor of 2, with corresponding mathematical expressions and graphical representations. Key time instants are tracked to verify the scaling effects.

figures.caption: Illustration of time scaling of a signal x(t) showing the original signal (a), compressed version x_c(t) = x(3t/2) (b), and expanded version x_e(t) = x(t/2) (c).
figures.description: The figure has three time-domain plots with horizontal axis t and signal labels x(t), x_c(t), and x_e(t). In (a), the original signal is zero outside a finite interval, equals 2 from t = -1.5 to 0, and then follows a decaying exponential labeled 2e^{-t/2} until t = 3. In (b), the compressed signal x_c(t) = x(3t/2) keeps the same amplitude but all time landmarks move closer to the origin, from -0.5 to 1, with exponential segment 2e^{-3t/2}. In (c), the expanded signal x_e(t) = x(t/2) keeps the same amplitude but stretches the time support from -3 to 6, with a slower decay labeled 2e^{-t/4}; students should notice that time scaling changes duration and decay rate, not vertical amplitude.
